actions.thought: I am holding 9s 2h in the Small Blind (Button) heads-up. This is statistically one of the worst starting hands in poker. Although I have position and am getting 4:1 pot odds to limp, the playability of this hand is extremely poor (low equity realization), and it does not block opponent value ranges effectively. Without any reads on the opponent's tendencies in Hand #1, attempting to steal or limp is negative EV. The standard and disciplined play is to fold.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Jack of Spades and Ten of Spades, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. It has great playability and equity. Standard strategy is to raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) to take the initiative and build a pot with a strong hand.

I am holding 9c 6s in the Big Blind facing a 3bb (300) raise from the Button. While heads-up ranges are wide, 96o is a bottom-tier hand with poor playability and significant reverse implied odds, especially out of position. The opponent's 3x sizing (larger than a standard min-raise) reduces my pot odds to 2:1, requiring 33% equity realization. Given the positional disadvantage and the weakness of the hand, I cannot profitably realize enough equity to justify a call. Folding is the standard play.

I am on the Button (SB) with [Ad 7d], which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play (top 20-30% range). It has high card value and flush potential. With 100BB stacks, standard strategy dictates opening with a raise rather than limping or folding. A min-raise to 200 allows me to capitalize on position, potentially take down the pot pre-flop, or play a pot in position with a hand that has good equity against a calling range.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit. The Button (Small Blind) has raised to 2x (200). K9o is a strong enough hand to defend my blind, especially getting 3:1 pot odds on a call. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity against a standard opening range from the button without bloating the pot unnecessarily early in the match.

I am in the Big Blind and was the caller pre-flop. The board has come Ace-high with an Ace of Spades and a spade flush draw. I have middle pair (Nines) with a strong King kicker. In heads-up play, middle pair is a solid hand but vulnerable. Since I am out of position and the board contains an Ace which hits the pre-flop raiser's range well, checking is the most standard and prudent play. It allows me to control the pot and see how my opponent reacts to this board.

I hold top pair (Aces) with a 7 kicker on a board of [As 9c 2s]. This is a strong hand in heads-up play, likely ahead of the opponent's Big Blind checking range. The board contains a flush draw (spades), so checking back allows the opponent to realize equity for free with draws or random overcards to the 9/2. I should bet for value to target worse Aces, 9x hands, and flush draws. A small-to-medium continuation bet (c-bet) is appropriate to keep the opponent's calling range wide while charging draws. I will bet 150 (approx 37% of the pot).

I have middle pair (Nines) with a King kicker on an Ace-high board. My opponent bet 150 into a 400 pot (roughly 1/3 pot size). This is a standard continuation bet from the pre-flop aggressor. While the Ace favors their range, middle pair is too strong to fold to a single small bet in a heads-up pot. Raising would likely only be called by better hands like an Ace, so calling is the best way to keep the pot controlled, realize my equity, and see how the turn develops.

On the turn, I have improved to trip Nines with a King kicker. While the 9s completes a possible spade flush, my hand is extremely strong in a heads-up pot. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. Checking is the most strategic move here; it allows me to control the pot size and provides an opportunity for my opponent to continue bluffing with hands that I beat or to value bet with an Ace. If I am raised, I can re-evaluate, but for now, checking to the aggressor is the standard and most flexible play.

On the Turn, the board is [9c As 2s 9s]. The 9s is a scare card that completes the flush draw and pairs the board, giving the opponent potential trips or a flush. My hand (Top Pair, weak kicker) has showdown value but is vulnerable. If I bet, worse hands likely fold and better hands (flush, trips, strong Ace) continue or raise. By checking back, I control the pot size, avoid getting check-raised, and can induce bluffs or comfortably call a reasonable bet on the river. I am pot controlling to get to showdown.

I have Trip Nines with a King kicker on a board of 9c-As-2s-9s-Jd. While the board has three spades, my opponent checked back on the turn when the third spade and the second nine appeared. This suggests they likely do not have a flush or were pot-controlling with a marginal hand like an Ace. Since I have a very strong hand in a heads-up situation, I will lead with a value bet on the river. I am targeting an Ace or a Jack that may have improved on the river. A bet of 450 into a 700 pot (approx. 64%) is a standard value sizing to extract chips from these weaker made hands.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a 7 kicker. The pot is 700 and the opponent leads for 450 on the river. The board is [9c As 2s 9s Jd]. The turn brought a third spade and paired the board (9s), making flushes and trips possible. However, I checked back the turn, which caps my range and often induces the opponent to bluff on the river with missed draws or air, or value bet worse hands like weaker Aces (A3-A6) thinking they are good. I am getting roughly 2.5:1 odds (need ~28% equity). In a heads-up scenario, folding Top Pair to a standard-sized river lead after showing weakness on the turn is too tight and exploitable. My hand is near the top of my perceived range. I will call to catch bluffs and split with/beat worse Aces.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with Jack of Hearts and Deuce of Spades. This is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop. While it is standard to play a wide range in heads-up play, Jh2s is near the bottom of the starting hand matrix. Given the current stack sizes and my position, I will choose to fold and wait for a more favorable hand to contest the pot.

In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, Ace-Ten offsuit is a very strong hand from the button (Small Blind). The standard play with the top portion of my range is to open-raise to build the pot and seize initiative. I will make a standard min-raise to 200 to put pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 8s. Pro has raised to 200 (a 2x open). The pot is currently 300, and it costs me 100 more to call. This gives me 3:1 pot odds. 98o is a standard defending hand in this position against a button open, as it has decent connectivity and equity against a wide range. I will call to see the flop.

I have top pair with a nine on a 9-3-3 board, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Since I am in the Big Blind and acting first post-flop, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows Pro to continuation bet with a wide range, including air or overcards, while I maintain control of the pot size and hide the strength of my hand.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit on a dry, paired board of 9-3-3. Being in position, I have the option to c-bet or check back. This board texture hits the Big Blind's calling range (which includes many 3x and 9x hands, as well as pocket pairs) somewhat reasonably, but also misses a lot of his high-card trash. My Ace-high has good showdown value. Betting folds out hands I crush (like K-high or Q-high) and keeps in hands that beat me or have good equity. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, avoid getting check-raised, and realize my equity. It also under-represents my hand strength, potentially inducing bluffs from the opponent on later streets. Therefore, checking is the prudent play.

On the turn, a Queen appears which is an overcard to my pair of nines. Since Pro checked back on the flop, it is less likely they have a monster hand like a set of threes, but the Queen is well within a pre-flop raiser's range. I will check again to see how Pro reacts to this card. This allows me to control the pot size and potentially catch a bluff or see a cheap showdown if the action remains passive.

On the Turn, the board is 9d 3s 3h Qs. After checking back the flop, the Queen is a card that favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor, but my specific hand (Ace-Ten) has decent showdown value. Betting here (a delayed c-bet) would likely fold out hands I am beating (King-high, Jack-high) and only get action from better hands (Qx, 3x, or sticky 9x/pocket pairs). By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep the opponent's range wide, potentially allowing me to catch a bluff on the river or win at showdown with Ace-high.

I have a pair of nines on a board that has been checked through both the flop and the turn. My hand has significant showdown value in this spot. Since the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked back twice, it is unlikely they have a monster or a Queen. By checking the river, I can induce a bluff from their missed overcards or simply take my hand to a cheap showdown. If Pro bets, I intend to call given the strength of my hand relative to the passive action.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit on a board of 9d-3s-3h-Qs-2d. The action has gone check-check on both the flop and turn, and the opponent has checked to me again on the river. My hand constitutes a pair of Threes with an Ace kicker (effectively Ace-high showdown value). Betting here has little utility: worse hands (King-high, Jack-high) will likely fold, meaning I extract no value, and better hands (any pair like 2x, 9x, Qx, or pocket pairs) will likely call, meaning I lose additional chips. By checking back, I realize my showdown equity and win the pot against all of the opponent's busted high-card hands.

42o is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Although I have the advantage of position post-flop as the button, the hand's equity is extremely poor. Rather than attempting a speculative raise or limping into a potentially dominated pot, I will choose the conservative route and fold, preserving my stack for better starting hands.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Q8o, which is a strong enough hand to open in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, ranking in the top ~37% of hands. My opponent has been somewhat passive pre-flop, defending their big blind by calling raises but also folding to aggression. A min-raise to 200 allows me to take the initiative, capitalize on my position post-flop, and potentially take down the pot immediately if the opponent folds. Limping is an option, but raising builds the pot with a decent hand and exerts pressure.

With 9h 8s in the Big Blind, I am facing a minimum raise to 200 from the Button. This gives me excellent pot odds (3:1) to defend. 98o has reasonable connectivity and can flop well enough to justify a call, even though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

On this very dry paired board of 5d 2h 2c, I have 9-high. Standard play in the Big Blind after defending pre-flop is to check to the aggressor. I will check and see if Pro decides to continuation bet or check back.

The flop falls 5d 2h 2c, an extremely dry and paired board. In a Heads-Up scenario, Queen-high (Q8o) has reasonable showdown value on this texture, as it is ahead of many un-paired hands in the opponent's defending range (like J-high, T-high, or suited connectors that missed). While a small continuation bet is a viable strategy to deny equity to overcards (like K-high), betting risks bloating the pot against pocket pairs, an Ace, or a Deuce. If I bet and get called, I am likely behind and in a difficult spot on the turn. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs on later streets or get to a cheap showdown. It also balances my checking range for when I have monster hands (like a full house) or total air.

The Ace on the turn is a scare card that theoretically favors the pre-flop aggressor, but Pro's check on the flop might indicate they don't have a strong hand. Regardless, with 9-high and no equity, I have no reason to bet here. I will check and see if Pro bets or checks behind again.

I hold Q8o on a board of 5d 2h 2c Ad. After checking back the flop, the turn brings an Ace, which favors my range as the pre-flop raiser but also connects well with the Big Blind's defending range. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, attempting to fold out better hands like King-high or small pocket pairs (33-66). However, given the opponent's passive tendencies shown in previous hands, they may check-call with an Ace or a 5. My Queen-high has some showdown value against missed random hands. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, see a free river, and potentially win at showdown without risking chips against a trapped Ace or trip Deuces.

The board has completed as 5d-2h-2c-Ad-3c. My 9-high has essentially no showdown value. However, Pro has checked back on both the flop and the turn, which indicates a very weak range, likely consisting of low pocket pairs or high-card hands like K-high or Q-high. While a bluff could potentially take down the pot, the board texture (specifically the Ace on the turn) makes it difficult to represent a strong hand convincingly after checking twice myself. I will check and see if Pro checks behind for a showdown or attempts a late steal, in which case I will likely fold.

I arrived at the river with Queen-high after checking back the flop and turn. The board reads 5d 2h 2c Ad 3c. The pot is small (400). My opponent checked to me on the river. The 3 on the river puts a possible straight on the board for any 4, and there is an Ace on the turn which hits the Big Blind's defending range heavily. While my opponent has shown passivity (checking all streets), attempting to bluff here is risky as they may look me up with any pair (2x, 3x, 5x) or a trapped Ace, given my checking line looks weak. My Queen-high has a sliver of showdown value against total air (like J-high or T-high missed connectors). Betting is unlikely to fold out better hands often enough to be profitable, and checking ensures I lose no further chips if I am behind.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the weakest hand in Texas Hold'em. While I am on the button in a heads-up match and could potentially complete the small blind or raise as a bluff, the hand has extremely poor playability and equity. Maintaining a disciplined range is more important at this stage of the tournament than trying to steal a small pot with the worst possible cards. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

In a Heads-Up match, J6o is a marginal hand, often falling into the bottom tier of playable hands. The opponent (Flash) has defended their Big Blind by calling every raise I've made so far (Hands 3, 5, 7), indicating a low fold-to-steal frequency. Since the opponent is sticky pre-flop and playability of J6o is poor, raising has low expected value as I likely won't generate immediate folds and will be playing a weak hand against a wide calling range. While limping is an option to see a cheap flop in position, folding is a disciplined low-variance approach to preserve chips for better spots, especially given the opponent's passive-calling tendencies.

I am on the button with 52s, which is a marginal hand but playable in a heads-up situation, especially when looking to maintain pressure or exploit post-flop. The pot is 150 and it costs 50 more to call, giving me 3:1 odds. However, I want to take control of the hand or potentially fold. Given the stacks and the stage, I'll complete the small blind to see a flop cheaply or fold if facing significant resistance later, but here I will just call the 50 to see a flop and use position to my advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s2s facing a limp from the Small Blind. This is a weak suited hand. Since I have the option to check and see a free flop, there is no strategic advantage to raising and bloating the pot out of position with a marginal holding. I will check to realize my equity for free.

I hold a flush draw (9s2s) on a Qs-8s-5d board. As the Big Blind in a heads-up limped pot, I am out of position post-flop. The standard line is to check to the Button (Small Blind). Leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal here because I would likely fold out worse hands that I want to stay in, and only get called by better hands (like pairs) that dominate my current 9-high. Checking preserves my range and gives me the option to call a bet based on pot odds or see a free turn card to hit my flush.

I have bottom pair (fives) on a dry board of Q-8-5. In a limped pot, this is a reasonable hand to take to a showdown or see a turn card cheaply. Since my opponent checked from the big blind, I will check back to control the size of the pot and avoid being check-raised off my equity. This allows me to see how the turn develops without committing more chips with a marginal kicker.

I hold 9s2s on a board of 8s-Qc-5d-Jd. I flopped a flush draw and turned a gutshot straight draw (need a Ten). The opponent checked back the flop, suggesting a weak range or pot control. Being out of position with a drawing hand and 9-high, my primary goal is to realize my equity cheaply. Leading out (semi-bluffing) has merit to take down the dead money, but if raised, I lose my equity. Checking is the safer, standard line to hopefully see a free river card or call a reasonable bet given my multiple draws.

I have bottom pair (fives) on a board of Q-8-5-J. The pot was limped pre-flop and checked on the flop. My opponent has checked again on the turn. While my pair of fives is likely the best hand often enough against a double-check, it is very marginal and doesn't benefit much from betting; better hands are unlikely to fold, and I'd prefer to reach showdown cheaply. I will check back to maintain pot control and see the river for free.

I arrive at the river with 9-high, having missed my flush draw and gutshot straight draw. The board runs out 8s-Qc-5d-Jd-6h. The action went check-check on both the flop and turn. The pot is very small (200 chips, 2 BBs). My opponent's range is capped but likely contains showdown value like Ace-high, King-high, or a weak pair (5x, 6x, 8x) that checked back for pot control. While I have zero showdown value, bluffing into a calling station line (check-back twice usually implies desire for cheap showdown) in a tiny pot is generally negative EV. I hold a 9, which is a key card for the straights (T9, 97), meaning I could theoretically represent them, but my passive line on the flop and turn makes a sudden river lead suspicious and unlikely to fold out a pair. I will cut my losses and check, fully expecting to lose at showdown.

I have bottom pair (fives) on a board of Q-8-5-J-6. The pot was limped pre-flop and checked through on every street. My opponent has checked to me on the river. While my pair of fives is likely the best hand given the passive action, there is little value in betting as worse hands will likely fold and better hands (like an 8 or a 6) will call. Checking back ensures I get to showdown and realize my equity in this small pot.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Q8o, which is a top 35-40% hand and well within a standard Heads-Up opening range. In Hand #7, I held the exact same hand (Q8o) and raised to 200, which resulted in a check-down pot. My opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to defend the Big Blind by calling rather than 3-betting, and plays somewhat passively post-flop. Raising to 200 allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or play in position against a likely weaker range. Folding is too tight for Q8o in a heads-up match.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in a heads-up situation, particularly when facing a button raise. I have the chip lead and want to apply pressure. A 3-bet to 600 allows me to take the initiative, build a value pot, and potentially win the hand pre-flop against the opponent's wider opening range.

I hold Q8o on the Button and opened to 200. The opponent in the Big Blind, who has previously been passive (mostly calling or checking), has 3-bet to 600. This is the first 3-bet I have faced from this opponent in 11 hands, suggesting a stronger range. I need 33% equity to call (400 to win 1200), but Q8o performs poorly against a standard 3-bet range (likely dominated by AQ, KQ, QJ, 88+) and suffers from reverse implied odds. Despite having position post-flop, the prudent play against a sudden show of strength from a passive player is to fold.

In a heads-up scenario, Ten-Seven offsuit is a standard hand to open from the button. I have a significant stack lead and want to maintain aggression to keep the opponent on the defensive, especially after successfully pushing them off a hand in Hand 11. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

Opponent raises to 300 (3bb) from the button. K7o is a marginal hand. While Kx has decent raw equity heads-up, defending against a 3x raise from out of position is difficult and often unprofitable due to equity realization issues. The pot odds require ~33% realized equity. K7o has ~45% raw equity against a standard button range, but realizing that equity out of position against a larger sizing is tough. Given the opponent has shown a mix of limping and folding, the 3x raise likely indicates a stronger range. I choose to fold to conserve chips and wait for a better spot or a smaller raise size.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with T8s, a suited connector that plays well in heads-up. Standard heads-up strategy dictates opening this hand. I have consistently raised to 200 with my playable hands (Hands #3, #5, #7, #11) and folded my trash. To maintain consistency and put pressure on the Big Blind while playing in position post-flop, I will raise to the minimum of 200.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Five of spades. The Button has raised to 200. Given the pot odds (calling 100 to win a pot of 300) and the strength of a suited King in heads-up play, this is a clear defense. While I could 3-bet to put pressure on the opener, K5s plays well post-flop, and calling allows me to keep the pot controlled while realizing my equity.

On a paired board of 8c-2c-2h, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor. My King-high has some showdown value and equity against a wide button opening range, but leading into the raiser on this board texture is suboptimal. I will check and see how the opponent chooses to act.

I hold Top Pair (Th 8h) on a paired board [8c 2c 2h]. While this is a strong hand in heads-up, my kicker is mediocre and the board contains a flush draw. Betting risks isolating myself against better hands (Trip Deuces, better Eights) or getting check-raised. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, induces bluffs from the opponent on future streets, and protects my checking range. I can comfortably call a turn bet or bet for value later.

The turn 7c brings a third club to the board. After both players checked the flop, the range of both players remains relatively wide, though Pro checking back on a paired board often indicates a hand with some showdown value or a total miss. My King-high has some showdown value but no club for a redraw. I will check here to see how Pro responds to the flush-completing card; if they check behind, I might be able to win at showdown or bluff on a favorable river. If they bet, I will likely fold given the lack of connectivity my hand has with this board.

I hold Top Pair (Th) on a board that is now very wet [8c 2c 2h 7c]. The 7c on the turn completes the flush draw, meaning there are three clubs on the board. My hand (Th 8h) has no clubs. While I have showdown value with top pair, I am currently behind any flush, any 2 (trips), or two pair. Betting here isolates me against hands that beat me (who will call or raise) and folds out worse hands. Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, realize my equity for free, and potentially induce bluffs on the river or call a reasonable bet on a safe river card.

On the river Jack of Spades, the board reads 8c-2c-2h-7c-Js. Both players have checked the flop and the turn, which heavily suggests a lack of strong hands in either range. My King-high has very little showdown value, but it does beat some total misses. However, since the pot is small and Pro has shown a willingness to check back marginal hands or misses, I will check to see if I can win at showdown or if they choose to bluff. If I bet, I am likely only getting called by better (pairs or Ace-high) and folding out hands I already beat. Checking is the most prudent play in this small pot.

I hold a pair of Eights (Th 8h) on a board of [8c 2c 2h 7c Js]. The river Jack is an overcard to my pair, downgrading my hand to second pair effectively (if the opponent has a Jack). The board also contains three clubs (flush possibility) and a possible straight connectivity (7-8-J on board plus my T means any 9 makes a straight). The opponent has checked three times. Betting here offers little value as worse hands (A-high, small pairs) will likely fold, while better hands (Jack, 9, Flush, 2) will call or raise. Checking back guarantees I realize my showdown value against weaker hands like A-high or smaller pocket pairs without risking chips in a spot where I am often behind when called.

I have a premium hand (KQs) on the button. This is a clear raising hand in heads-up play to build value and take control of the pot. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate here to test the Big Blind's strength and set up a post-flop advantage.

I am holding Qd 3c in the Big Blind. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3 BB) from the Button. This is a larger open size than a min-raise, which worsens my pot odds. I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 600, requiring about 33% equity. While Q3o has reasonable raw equity against a wide range, it suffers from very poor playability out of position. I will likely under-realize my equity. Flash has shown a tendency to fold some buttons, so their range is not 100% wide. Against a standard or even slightly wide opening range, defending Q3o against a 3x raise is a losing play. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I hold Ten-Three offsuit (T3o) on the Button. In heads-up play, while ranges are wide, T3o is one of the weaker hands in the range (roughly bottom 15-20%) and has poor playability due to the gap and lack of flush potential. The opponent (Flash) has shown aggression by 3-betting previously (Hand #11) and raising buttons consistently. With 88 big blinds deep, I want to avoid inflating the pot with a hand that is easily dominated and hard to navigate post-flop. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.

I am on the button with Qs 5d. Observing the previous hands, the opponent (Pro) has folded to my pre-flop raises in Hand #2, #12, and #14. Q5o is a marginal hand, but in heads-up play, it is strong enough to attempt a steal from the button, especially since the opponent has shown a high frequency of folding to 3x raises. I will raise to 300 to continue this pressure and take down the blinds or play the pot in position.

Opponent raises to 300 (3x), which has been their standard sizing. I hold 9c6c, a suited one-gapper. In heads-up play, the button's range is wide, and my hand has sufficient equity (approx 38-40% vs a 50% range) to call getting 2:1 pot odds (need 33% equity). I folded the offsuit version of this hand earlier to the same raise size, but the suitedness adds enough value to make this a defend to prevent over-folding the Big Blind.

I hold bottom pair (6s) on an A-T-6 rainbow flop. The board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which contains many Ax and Tx combinations. Donk betting here would likely only get called by better hands and fold out worse ones, which is inefficient. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or catch a bluff. The standard play is to check to the raiser to see if they continue. If they check back, I realize equity; if they bet, I will evaluate based on sizing.

On this Ace-high board (Ad 6h Ts), I have no equity with my Queen-high and five-kicker. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this texture hits a wide variety of the Big Blind's calling range (any Ace, any Ten, or middling pairs). Since the opponent checked to me, checking back allows me to control the pot size and see a free turn card, as a continuation bet is unlikely to fold out any better hands on such a static, high-card board.

I hold two pair (Nines and Sixes) on a board of Ad 6h Ts 9d. My opponent, the pre-flop raiser, checked back the flop, which caps their range and suggests they likely hold weak Ax, Tx, underpairs, or air, rather than a strong Ace or set. The turn card brings straight possibilities (78) and flush draws, making the board wetter. Giving a free card here is risky as many river cards (J, Q, K, 7, 8, Diamond) could kill my action or improve the opponent. Given the opponent's tendency to check back marginal hands in previous hands, I must bet to extract value from Ax/Tx and charge draws. A probe bet of half-pot (300) is appropriate to get value from worse hands while denying equity.

The opponent has led out for 300 into a 600 pot on the turn after the flop was checked through. My hand, Queen-high with a five kicker, has no showdown value and no draw on this Ace-high board. Since I have no equity and the opponent is showing strength or at least interest in the pot, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 5s 3h, a very weak offsuit hand. My opponent has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind frequently against my raises (calling in Hands 3, 5, 7, 13) and has even 3-bet once (Hand 11). While I have position post-flop, 53o plays very poorly and has low equity. I have previously folded hands like T3o and J6o in this position, so folding 53o is consistent with my strategy of trimming the bottom of my opening range to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots.

I am on the button with Qh 4c. While this is a weak hand, in heads-up play, it is often profitable to open-raise to put pressure on the big blind. Pro has shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop raises in previous hands (Hands 2, 12, 14). I will raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds immediately. If called, I will evaluate the flop carefully given the weakness of my hand.

I am holding 4h 5c in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. My opponent has consistently used this larger sizing when opening. To call, I need to contribute 200 chips into a final pot of 600, requiring 33% equity. While 54o has some connectivity, it is at the very bottom of my range and performs poorly out of position, especially against a larger open size which worsens my pot odds. Defending here is likely negative EV compared to folding.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o), which is statistically one of the weakest hands in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em. With 89 big blinds effective, playing this hand essentially relies entirely on fold equity. Given the opponent has shown a willingness to defend their Big Blind (calling 200 raises in previous hands like #3, #5, #7, #13) and 3-bet (Hand #11), opening with trash like T2o is negative EV.
